title: The UDF tax: one transform, three prices

steps.narration: The task is trivial: uppercase the country column on 40 million order rows for a downstream join key. Three engineers reach for three different tools. Same output, wildly different bills — and the difference is entirely about how many times data crosses the JVM↔Python boundary.

Option A — the built-in. F.upper(F.col("country")). This never leaves the JVM: Catalyst compiles it, Tungsten runs it on the off-heap columnar buffers, whole stages code-generated. The rows stay in Spark's own memory the entire time.

state.note: zero boundary crossings — data stays in Tungsten's columnar format

steps.narration: The executors hum: 16 cores stream columnar batches through generated code, all bars near full and even. Nobody is waiting on anybody. This is the baseline every other option is measured against.

Option B — the Python UDF. @udf; def up(s): return s.upper(). Functionally identical, and 30× slower. Here is why: for every one of 40 million rows, Spark must serialize the value, ship it over a local socket to a Python worker process, deserialize it, run your one line, then serialize the result back and deserialize it into the JVM. Forty million round trips.

state.note: ☕ this is the JNI-boundary tax: cheap logic, ruinous crossing cost — same shape as a native call per element

steps.narration: Watch the executors during the UDF stage: the JVM side is starved, stalled waiting for the Python worker to hand rows back one at a time. Green means the pipe; the hot bars are the Python processes pegged at 100% doing nothing but pickling. The cluster is busy being idle.

state.note: serialization dominates; the actual .upper() is a rounding error in the total

steps.narration: Option C — the pandas UDF. @pandas_udf; def up(s: pd.Series) -> pd.Series: return s.str.upper(). Same Python, same logic — but now Spark ships rows in Arrow batches of ~10,000, not one at a time. The boundary is crossed ~4,000 times instead of 40 million, and the crossing itself is zero-copy columnar Arrow, not per-value pickling.

state.note: same boundary, ~10,000× fewer crossings — vectorized, zero-copy Arrow instead of per-row pickle

steps.narration: Executors during the pandas-UDF stage: the Python workers now do real vectorized work on whole Series at once, and the JVM side keeps pace feeding Arrow batches. Utilization is high and balanced again — not built-in-fast, but the same order of magnitude, not the same universe as the row UDF.

The three prices, side by side: 1.2s built-in · 38s Python UDF · 4.5s pandas UDF. The decision rule writes itself. Reach for a built-in or F.expr first — there is one for far more than people expect. If the logic genuinely can't be expressed in built-ins, use a pandas UDF, never a plain row-at-a-time @udf.

state.note: order of preference: built-in / expr → pandas UDF → (almost never) row-at-a-time UDF

steps.narration: The trap that makes this a field-notes story rather than a footnote: at 40 thousand rows in a dev notebook, all three finish in well under a second and look identical. The 30× gap only opens at production scale — so the row UDF passes review, passes staging, and detonates the SLA at 3 a.m. on the first full-volume run. Cost is a property of the boundary, and the boundary is invisible until the data is big.

state.note:
dev (40k rows): 0.02s / 0.09s / 0.03s — indistinguishable
prod (40M rows): 1.2s / 38s / 4.5s — a paging incident
